title: Floor-Standing Upholstered Headboard
summary: A padded, fabric-wrapped headboard built like a small stud-wall frame — stands on the floor behind any bed.
description:
An upholstered headboard is usually sold as a single expensive unit that only fits one bed size. This one is a small 2x4 stud-wall frame, faced in thin plywood, padded, and wrapped in fabric — sized to whatever width you actually need, up to 96" for a king.

Because it **stands on the floor rather than mounting to a bed frame**, it works behind any bed you own now or buy later. The frame does all the structural work; the plywood face, padding, and fabric are just the finish layer on top of a box anyone can build in an afternoon.

Sized here for a queen bed (62" wide); the frame scales directly for any width — just adjust the two horizontal rails and the plywood panel to match.

tools.note: Or a table saw — mostly straight crosscuts and one plywood panel to size.
Attaches the padding and stretches the fabric over it.

materials.name: 1/4" plywood or wood paneling, 4x8 sheet
materials.note: Cut down to the frame's face size.

materials.name: 1-1/4" screws or staples
materials.note: Attaches the plywood panel to the frame.

cutList.note: Adjust to your headboard's desired width, up to 96in.
Height minus 3in — adjust to your desired finished height, up to 48in.
For securing the finished headboard to the wall or bed frame.

steps.title: Build the frame
steps.body:
Build a rectangular 2x4 frame sized to your desired headboard width and height, using the two rails and four vertical studs, fastened with the 2-3/4" screws.

Square the frame before you drive the last few screws — an out-of-square frame telegraphs straight through the plywood, padding, and fabric to the finished piece.

steps.title: Attach the mounting cleats
steps.body: Attach the two mounting cleats to the back of the frame now, while it's easy to get a drill square to the work. These are what let you secure the finished headboard to a wall or bed frame later.

steps.title: Attach the face panel
steps.body: Cut the plywood panel to the frame's outer size and fasten it to the front of the frame with glue and screws or staples.

steps.title: Add the padding
steps.body: Layer batting (or old comforters, doubled up) over the face panel and staple it to the back of the frame, pulling it snug but not so tight it compresses flat — you want a soft dome, not a hard board.

steps.title: Stretch and staple the fabric
steps.body:
Center the fabric over the padded panel. Staple the bottom edge first, then pull taut and staple the top, then the sides, working from the center of each edge outward.

Fold each corner neatly like wrapping a gift box rather than bunching it — a bunched corner is the one detail that gives away a home upholstery job at a glance.

steps.title: Secure it in place
steps.body: Stand the finished headboard behind the bed and secure it to the wall with a French cleat, or fasten it to the bed frame itself through the mounting cleats, so it can't tip forward.
